title: ISMP API Server Down or Unhealthy
summary: The ISMP API server (ismp-apiserver, port 8443) is not responding or failing health checks. All VM, storage, network, and auth operations will fail. The UI will show connection errors.
steps: Check pod status: kubectl get pods -n ismp-system -l app=ismp-apiserver
Check endpoint health: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver -- curl -sk https://localhost:8443/healthz
Check recent logs for Gin framework errors: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver --tail=100
Look for 'failed to initialize' errors in logs (OIDC, Prometheus client — non-blocking but indicate config issues)
Check if Prometheus URL is reachable: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver -- curl http://prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.monitoring.svc:9090/-/healthy
Check TLS certificate validity if using custom certs: kubectl get secret -n ismp-system | grep tls
If CrashLoopBackOff: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver --previous
Check resource pressure: kubectl top pods -n ismp-system
Verify JWT signing secret is present: kubectl get secret -n ismp-system | grep ismp-jwt
Check kubernetes events: kubectl get events -n ismp-system --sort-by=lastTimestamp | tail -20
causes: JWT signing secret missing or corrupted
TLS certificate expired for port 8443
Prometheus sidecar URL unreachable (non-fatal but causes 500s on /metrics endpoints)
OOMKilled due to 256Mi memory limit under high request load
OIDC provider config invalid (blocks startup if required)
All 3 replicas on same node — node failure takes down API

title: ISMP Operator Reconciliation Failure
summary: The ISMP operator controllers are failing to reconcile CRDs (VMs, Volumes, LoadBalancers). VMs may not start, volumes may not attach, and load balancers may not provision. Operator runs on control-plane nodes only.
steps: Check operator pod status: kubectl get pods -n ismp-system -l app=ismp-operator
Check operator health probe: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator -- curl -s http://localhost:8081/healthz
Check readiness: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator -- curl -s http://localhost:8081/readyz
Read operator logs: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator --tail=200
Look for controller-specific errors — 'VirtualMachineReconciler', 'VolumeAttachmentReconciler', 'LoadBalancerReconciler'
Check if KubeVirt is healthy: kubectl get pods -n kubevirt
Check if Rook-Ceph is healthy: kubectl get cephcluster -A && kubectl get pods -n rook-ceph | grep -v Running
Check if MetalLB is healthy (for LoadBalancer controller): kubectl get pods -n metallb-system
Verify leader election is working (if enabled): kubectl get lease -n ismp-system
Check ISMP CRD status for stuck resources: kubectl get virtualmachine,volume,loadbalancer -A
Check controller events: kubectl get events -n ismp-system --field-selector reason=FailedReconcile
causes: KubeVirt unavailable — VM controller cannot create/modify VirtualMachine objects
Rook-Ceph degraded — Volume controller cannot provision or attach block devices
MetalLB missing IP pool — LoadBalancer controller cannot allocate external IPs
RBAC permission revoked for ismp-operator ServiceAccount
CRD schema mismatch after upgrade — reconcile loop panics
Template referenced in VirtualMachine spec does not exist (ISMPVMTemplate not found)
Leader election lock stuck after operator crash — another replica cannot take over

title: ISMP VM Failed or High VM Failure Rate
summary: One or more ISMP VirtualMachine CRDs are in Failed state, or >10% of VMs are failing (ISMPVMHighFailureRate alert). Maps to KubeVirt VMI objects under the hood.
steps: List failed ISMP VMs: kubectl get virtualmachine -A -o wide | grep -v Running
Get ISMP VM details: kubectl describe virtualmachine <name> -n <ns>
Find the corresponding KubeVirt VMI: kubectl get vmi -n <ns>
Describe the VMI for KubeVirt-level events: kubectl describe vmi <name> -n <ns>
Check the virt-launcher pod: kubectl get pods -n <ns> -l kubevirt.io/vm=<name>
Read virt-launcher logs: kubectl logs -n <ns> -l kubevirt.io/vm=<name>
Check if the VM uses a VolumeAttachment — verify volumes are attached: kubectl get volumeattachment -n <ns>
Check if the boot image exists: kubectl get virtualmachineimage -A | grep <image-name>
Check if the template is valid: kubectl get virtualmachinetemplate -A | grep <template>
Check node resources where VM was scheduled: kubectl describe node <node-name> | grep -A5 Allocated
Check ISMP cluster health for systemic issues: kubectl get clusterhealth -A
Check ISMP operator logs for VM reconcile errors: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator | grep VirtualMachine
causes: Boot disk/image not available or RBD volume not attached
Insufficient CPU/memory on the scheduled node (KubeVirt live migration may help)
VirtualMachineTemplate referenced does not exist or has changed
KubeVirt version mismatch with ISMP operator
VolumeAttachment controller failed to mount Ceph RBD block device to node
VM migration failed due to network or storage issue
Node running VM went NotReady — VM should live-migrate but may get stuck

title: ISMP Disk Health Warning or Critical
summary: ISMPDiskFailing or ISMPDiskFailed alert firing. DiskHealth CRD shows a disk in Warning or Critical state based on SMART data. May indicate imminent hardware failure.
steps: List disk health status: kubectl get diskhealth -A -o wide
Describe failing disk: kubectl describe diskhealth <name> -n <ns>
Check which node the disk is on from the DiskHealth spec
Check SMART details from the status conditions on the DiskHealth object
Check Ceph OSD status for that disk: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd status
Check Ceph overall health: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status
If Ceph OSD is down: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd tree
Check if data is being rebalanced away from the failing OSD: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph pg stat
Check if any volumes use this disk: kubectl get volume -A | grep <node-name>
For critical disks, check if Ceph has enough replicas: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd pool ls detail
Check ISMP monitoring API: curl -sk -H 'Authorization: Bearer <jwt>' https://<ismp-api>/api/v1/disk-health
causes: Physical disk developing bad sectors (SMART reallocated sector count increasing)
Disk temperature exceeding threshold
NVMe wear indicator approaching end-of-life
Ceph OSD crashed due to underlying disk I/O errors
Disk controller firmware issue causing intermittent failures
RAID controller reporting disk failure in pass-through mode

title: ISMP Node Not Ready
summary: ISMPNodeNotReady or ISMPAllNodesDown alert firing. ISMP NodeHealth CRD shows node(s) not Ready. VMs on the node may be evicted or stuck.
steps: Check ISMP node health: kubectl get nodehealth -A -o wide
Describe affected node health: kubectl describe nodehealth <name> -n <ns>
Check Kubernetes node status: kubectl describe node <node-name>
Look for pressure conditions: MemoryPressure, DiskPressure, PIDPressure in node conditions
Check kubelet status via ISMP diagnostic (if node is accessible): check systemctl status kubelet via run_diagnostic_cmd
Check if VMs were live-migrated away: kubectl get vmi -A -o wide | grep <node-name>
Check if node is in maintenance mode via ISMP API: GET /api/v1/nodes/<name>/status
Check node metrics: kubectl top node <node-name>
Check kube-ovn connectivity from the node: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide | grep <node-name>
Check ISMP cluster health: kubectl get clusterhealth -A
Check recent events: kubectl get events -A --field-selector involvedObject.name=<node-name>
causes: Kubelet crashed or stopped on the node
Network partition — node cannot reach API server (kube-ovn issue on Flatcar)
Disk pressure from container images or log accumulation (Flatcar /var on tmpfs for PXE-booted nodes)
Memory pressure from VMs exceeding host memory
Ceph OSD on node consuming all memory/CPU
Node kernel panic or hardware crash
BMC power cycle triggered outside of ISMP (manual intervention)

title: ISMP Volume or Storage Failing
summary: ISMPVolumesFailing alert firing, or Volume/VolumeAttachment CRDs stuck in error state. Block storage backed by Ceph RBD is unavailable.
steps: List ISMP volumes in error: kubectl get volume -A | grep -v Ready
Describe failing volume: kubectl describe volume <name> -n <ns>
Check VolumeAttachment status if a VM is trying to mount: kubectl get volumeattachment -A
Check Ceph overall health: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status
Check Ceph OSD status: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph osd status
Check if Ceph pool is full: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph df
Check RBD image exists: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- rbd ls <pool>
Check if PVC exists for the volume: kubectl get pvc -n <ns>
Check Rook-Ceph operator logs for provisioning errors: kubectl logs -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-operator --tail=100
Check if CSI driver is healthy: kubectl get pods -n rook-ceph | grep csi
Check ISMP operator volume controller logs: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator | grep -i volume
causes: Ceph cluster in HEALTH_WARN or HEALTH_ERR state (OSD down, PG degraded)
Ceph pool at capacity — cannot allocate new RBD images
Rook-Ceph CSI driver not running (csi-rbdplugin DaemonSet)
Network partition between node and Ceph storage network (bond1 / storage_ip down)
RBD image locked by another process (VM ungraceful shutdown without releasing lock)
StorageClass pointing to non-existent Ceph pool
VolumeAttachment timeout — Ceph map operation timed out on node

title: ISMP Cluster Health Degraded or Critical
summary: ISMPClusterDegraded or ISMPClusterCritical alert is firing. The ClusterHealth CRD shows overall platform health is below acceptable threshold. Multiple subsystems may be affected.
steps: Get cluster health overview: kubectl get clusterhealth -A -o yaml
Check all ISMP components: kubectl get pods -n ismp-system
Check node health summary: kubectl get nodehealth -A
Check disk health summary: kubectl get diskhealth -A
Check VM failure rate: kubectl get virtualmachine -A | grep -v Running | wc -l
Check volume failures: kubectl get volume -A | grep -v Ready
Check ISMP monitoring API for full picture: GET /api/v1/cluster-health
Check Prometheus for underlying metrics: query ismp_cluster_health_status
Check if this correlates with a recent deployment: kubectl rollout history deploy -n ismp-system
Check all Kubernetes node status: kubectl get nodes
Check Ceph health: kubectl exec -n rook-ceph deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status
Look for cascading failures — start with nodes, then Ceph, then VMs
causes: Multiple node failures creating quorum loss in Ceph MONs
Cascading disk failures exceeding Ceph replica count
Network partition splitting cluster into two segments
etcd quorum lost — Kubernetes API degraded
Mass VM migration triggered by node failure consuming all resources
Recent ISMP operator upgrade with CRD incompatibility

title: ISMP Authentication Failure (JWT / OIDC / LDAP)
summary: Users cannot log in to ISMP. API returning 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden. May affect JWT, OIDC (Azure AD, Google), or LDAP/AD login.
steps: Test login API directly: curl -sk -X POST https://<ismp-api>/api/v1/auth/login -d '{"username":"admin","password":"..."}' | jq .status
Check API server logs for auth errors: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver | grep -i 'auth\|jwt\|oidc\|ldap\|forbidden'
Check if OIDC config CRD is valid: kubectl get oidcconfig -A -o yaml
Check if LDAP config CRD is valid: kubectl get ldapconfig -A -o yaml
Test OIDC provider reachability from API pod: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver -- curl -s <oidc-issuer>/.well-known/openid-configuration
Test LDAP reachability: kubectl exec -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-apiserver -- nc -zv <ldap-host> 389
Check if JWT signing secret is present and unchanged: kubectl get secret -n ismp-system | grep jwt
Check if admin user exists: kubectl get ismpuser -A | grep admin
Check token expiry — default access token may be short-lived; test refresh: POST /api/v1/auth/refresh
Check if RBAC role assignment is correct: kubectl get ismpuser <name> -o yaml | grep role
causes: JWT signing secret rotated without invalidating existing sessions
OIDC provider (Azure AD, Google) certificate changed or provider URL changed
LDAP/AD server unreachable or certificate expired
Clock skew between API server pods and OIDC provider >5 minutes
ISMPUser CRD deleted accidentally
OIDC config CRD has wrong clientID or clientSecret after IdP app re-registration
LDAP bind password changed in AD but not updated in LDAPConfig CRD

title: ISMP Load Balancer Not Provisioning
summary: ISMP LoadBalancer CRD is stuck in Pending/Error state. External IP not assigned. Services using ISMP LoadBalancers are not reachable externally.
steps: List ISMP load balancers: kubectl get loadbalancer -A
Describe the failing LB: kubectl describe loadbalancer <name> -n <ns>
Check corresponding Kubernetes Service: kubectl get svc -n <ns> | grep <name>
Check if MetalLB is healthy: kubectl get pods -n metallb-system
Check MetalLB IP pool availability: kubectl get ipaddresspool -n metallb-system
Check MetalLB L2 advertisement or BGP config: kubectl get l2advertisement,bgppeer -n metallb-system
Check MetalLB speaker logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l app=metallb,component=speaker --tail=50
Check MetalLB controller logs: kubectl logs -n metallb-system -l app=metallb,component=controller --tail=50
Check ISMP operator LB controller logs: kubectl logs -n ismp-system deploy/ismp-operator | grep -i loadbalancer
Check EndpointSlice created by ISMP: kubectl get endpointslice -n <ns> | grep <name>
Verify Kube-OVN is routing traffic correctly: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-ovn
causes: MetalLB IP address pool exhausted — no free IPs in the configured range
MetalLB speaker not running on node where endpoints live
Kube-OVN subnet conflict with MetalLB IP pool
ISMP LoadBalancer CRD requesting IP outside the configured MetalLB pool
L2 advertisement disabled on the network segment (MetalLB L2Advertisement resource missing)
EndpointSlice not created because ISMP operator LB controller is failing
